q3: The model kept treating the final sentence as a place to introduce new scope rather than drawing out what the paragraph's own logic had already implied but left unresolved. It understood the structural goal intellectually but kept defaulting to appended qualifications rather than earned forward dependencies — the distinction between those two things took many iterations to land.
q5: The rubric captured most of what I care about, and 'Inter-Paragraph Threading' in particular named the problem I kept flagging with the ending. That said, the rubric framed it as a craft principle, and what I actually experience is more visceral — a paragraph ending either holds me forward or releases me, and the model needed to feel that distinction, not just apply a rule about it. So the rubric helped, but it didn't fully close the gap.
q6: Seeing the rubric made me realize the model was consistently stronger on the opening-sentence orientation than on the closing-sentence dependency — it had clearly internalized that paragraphs need to declare their purpose upfront, but it hadn't internalized that endings need to be earned by the paragraph's own internal logic rather than appended. Without the rubric, I might have attributed all my frustration to the ending alone; with it, I could see the asymmetry more clearly.

q3: The main recurring issue was register consistency — the model defaulted to maintaining a uniform formal tone throughout, which technically satisfies academic writing norms but misses the strategic value of a well-placed plain-language moment. It took explicit prompting to get that informal aside in, and even then I had to approve the specific phrasing rather than the model intuiting where that seam should be.
q5: I did look at the rubric, and it captured a reasonable portion of what I care about — the argumentative spine criterion and the inter-paragraph threading criterion are genuinely close to how I think about section-level structure. What it missed is the internal rhythm within paragraphs, particularly the motivation-approach-detail-payoff sequence I want in methods writing. The rubric thinks at the paragraph-boundary level more than the intra-paragraph level.
q6: The rubric made me notice that the model is actually quite good at paragraph-opening orientation when it's explicitly scaffolded toward it — the third paragraph's opening landed well and I think the rubric's priority-one criterion was driving that. What it also surfaced is that the model tends to satisfy the threading criterion through explicit backward reference rather than genuine logical dependency, which is a subtler failure that the rubric gestures at but doesn't quite name precisely enough to correct it.

q3: The model had a persistent tendency to frame scope limitations as concessions rather than conclusions — that 'only that' construction kept surfacing in different forms, and I had to push on it explicitly each time. It understood the argumentative structure well but kept defaulting to a slightly defensive posture at the moments where the argument should have felt most settled.
q5: The rubric captured most of what I care about — the argumentative spine criterion and the inter-paragraph threading criterion in particular are close to how I actually think about paragraph structure. What it missed is the rhythmic quality I want in methods writing specifically: motivation, then approach, then detail, then payoff. The rubric treats paragraph openings as orientation devices, which is right, but doesn't capture that the internal sequence within a paragraph matters too.
q6: Seeing the rubric made me notice that the model was actually quite consistent on citation-ready architecture — it never once produced a sentence that would require restructuring to accept a reference, and I had taken that for granted. What I wouldn't have noticed without the rubric is that the tonal calibration criterion may have been what pushed the model toward the 'Put plainly' moment, but the rubric frames it as 'slight edge' when what I actually wanted was plain-language clarity — those are subtly different instincts and the model navigated it well enough, but I can see where that criterion could go wrong with a different draft.
